focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

By January the Mercer suit hung in its plastic cover behind Laci's work jackets, too good for the room and useless in it. Beni went out at dusk in dark trousers with shine at the knees, Noor's spare fleece over a thin jumper, and the cheap black shoes he had once kept for wet weather. Damp plaster sweated through the walls. Old onion oil from too many nights' frying sat in the curtains. Water had dried in crooked lines on the window and started again with the next rain. Laci sat at the table in thermal socks, rubbing white dust from the seam of his thumbnail with the edge of a Metro receipt.

"Night airport?" Laci asked.

"Yeah."

Laci grunted, not kind and not hostile either. He reached beside his chair and pushed over a dented red thermos with a sticker half torn off it, some cartoon excavator still visible in the scrape. "Take it. Machine coffee there tastes like burned screws."

Beni looked at him.

"Don't make a face," Laci said. "It's tea. I'm not giving you my rakia."

"I'm not making a face."

"You are. Go. If you miss the last bit of A406 before it clogs, you save twenty minutes."

Laci had never before offered him road advice without first attaching a lecture about rent. Beni unscrewed the thermos cap. Strong black tea hit up out of it with lemon peel and too much sugar. The sweetness made his teeth ache before he even drank it.

"I can give you one-fifty Friday," Beni said.

Laci kept scraping the receipt edge under his nail. "Good."

"That still leaves-"

"I know what it leaves." Laci shrugged into a hoodie dark with rain and powdered with plaster. "I'm not a bank statement."

Beni nodded. It was the nearest thing to mercy he was likely to get. He put the thermos in his backpack and went down the stairs past the bicycles with no front wheels an...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...the work civilized, but the order of it was already plain enough: wait here, bring this, not yet, keep that off paper. It came with a room off the kitchen and cash on Fridays. His name would stay where no one important could trip over it.

The pull stayed in him because he could already see the room: shutters closed, towel warm, mattress made for an adult spine. Tottenham would still be damp. Laci would still want money. Mercer would still be Mercer. Eszter still might never touch him again except by accident over a tray. Refusing Nina would not sweeten any of that. It would only leave him standing in the life he already had, without her better upholstery laid over it.

He stood there long enough for the landing radiator to click on by his shin.

He rubbed the rope groove at the base of his finger and looked at the paint worn off the sill by other hands. It would be another city, another corridor, another woman calling for the car. He knew the job already.

Downstairs a radio snapped alive. "Front gate open in two. Car on approach."

Beni straightened, one hand still on the paint-chipped sill. He did not say the answer out loud because there was no one there to hear it. He only let it settle into the place where he usually kept route numbers and night addresses.

He would not go to Paris, not after the speeches, not on Monday, not into a better room built around the same arrangement.

The first donor bell sounded upstairs, neat and resonant through the house. In the mews somebody swore with helpless fury about the missing van keys. Beni went down the rest of the stairs, crossed the service corridor, and took the Mercedes keys from the hook board. When Mrs. Keene's voice came over the radio asking for the car at the front, he answered at once and kept the no to himself.
